speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we, we got not too bad of food. It was prepared nicely. But it wasn't enough. We were hungry. You know, it wasn't enough. So the next day, when I went to collect my food, instead of getting my little portion of bread and my soup, the girl probably recognized me, that I had my punishment, she pushed a whole bread in front of my soup. And I put that whole bread underneath my jacket and soup, and I went home. And I looked at that girl, and she was crying. Well, anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happens next?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was only a few more weeks, I believe. And a delegation came and asked, we need machinists. We need electricians. And we need carpenters. Who is one of these three? Please step forward. So I stepped forward. I'm electrician. I was 17 years old. I'm electrician. So and they brought us with nice buses. They brought us to Blechhammer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blechhammer, that must be about, about I would say, maybe not more than 50 kilometer from Katowice. It's all in that same district. Because this is a district what has a lot of coal, and very fatty coal, actually-- number one great coal. And that's what they did in Blechhammer. They brought coals, and coals, and coals, and coals every day-- wagons, and wagons, and wagons full of coal. And what they did with the coal will surprise you. They did everything with the coal. They made rubber. They made chocolate. They made the confiture. And they made gasoline, and oil, and they made everything. And that Blechhammer must have been between 10 and 40 kilometer long. But it only was maybe one and a half to two kilometer wide. So a long, long strip through a forest, both sides was with the evergreen trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Blechhammer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in, I stayed in Blechhammer till 1944 in winter. Now, Blechhammer, in Blechhammer, I, I was lucky again. Because the next day, they brought us to the workplace. And they divided electrician into groups. Who is electrician technician and who is electrician for building? I said I'm electrician technician. So they, there were a few people. They sent us to another group, to another place. We went there and all of a sudden, there came the buses, the big mechanics.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of them was wearing a SS uniform with the death head on his cap and on his epaulettes. And I say to myself, I hope I'm not gonna get him. And he marched around. And he looked at me. And he said, oh, you, come on out. So, what can I do? I had to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he turned out to be the nicest man I've ever seen. He taught me electricity. And he taught me everything possible for the trade. And on top of that, he was the only one what had a key to a special part of the whole building, which was a powerhouse. And in that building, there was a part which filtered the smoke what goes out the smoke stacks. So that the flyers what come, the American or the English, cannot see the smoke. It filtered that. And inside, in that building, the electricity where the smoke goes through must have been at least two million volt. And the plates with that load and electricity attracted the particles, the-- what goes out on the chimney and collects it in that, on the plates. And what we had to do is every so often, to go and push a button so that an electric hammer is hammering these particles down into the basement.

speaker: interviewee
text: So because of that high voltage, nobody could have a key to go in there. Only he had the key. So he had the key, I had the key. And because I had the key, I could help a lot of people. Anybody who had a toothache, anybody who had a headache, anybody who didn't feel good, I told him that this and this person is gonna go, you know. Oh yeah, he says, fine, fine, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I went, opened up the door, and let these people in, in a special room, in a special room. And I said to them, look, don't go out from this room till I come to get you. If you go out of this room and you go anywhere near this [? other, ?] I will have no more privilege and probably I'm gonna get killed. You know, for doing that. I never had any troubles. And it was nice. And he was a very nice man. And he was from Berlin. And when he went for Christmas, he came back and he brought me a sweater, what I had to wear underneath my shirt. And he brought me cake, Christmas cake.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed together till almost the end, 1944, when we had to start the, the death march. And I said to him, I said, look, I didn't know we gonna start the death march. Because I said that, he said to me, and he said, you know what, I won't come tomorrow. I'm gonna run away. I said, why are you gonna run away? The Russians are here and I don't want me to catch. I said, why don't you stay? You stay with me. You're free. All the other prisoners, they know you were giving them the place, you know, when they had headaches and toothaches, and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no, no, no, no, you don't understand. I have a mark underneath my arm. That is my blood type. And that is tattooed. And I cannot take that off. And everybody knows that all the SS have that tattoo. I cannot stay. He didn't stay. The next day, we came to work. He wasn't there. And we only worked a half a day and then we marched back to the, to, to the barracks. And they started, you know, we go on the death march. But I want to tell you a little story about [? sadism ?] also in Blechhammer. One evening after we came home, one SS man, he came to our barrack. And he said your barrack was filthy and dirty, and you gonna get punished. And he said all the clothes off.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were used to that already, all the clothes off. So all the clothes off. So outside, march. We marched in the back of the barracks. There was an [? appeal ?] place. I don't know how you call it-- assemble, assemble place. And that was just sand, just plain sand. And he said lay down. So we all had to lay down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he said crawl like a snake. Well, crawl like a snake, everybody except two of us. There was a boy. He was from, from France, from Paris. He was not circumcised and I wasn't circumcised. It didn't bother us. But oh boy, these poor man and boys, it was terrible, you know. It was bleeding. They had to go and had the bandage done and everything. Yeah, and he was laughing. And he was, and if somebody wasn't laying down and crawling like a snake, he had the whip and whipped on the, in the back, you know. So anyhow

speaker: interviewer
text: The death march.

speaker: interviewee
text: The death march. That was the 22nd of January, 1945. We got assembled and each one got a, a blanket plus our clothes and shoes. And we marched. Most of these people what were not electrician, but were just laborers and working in other, they had this striped uniform and they have wooden shoes with the cloth upper, with you know, laces. I had leather shoes. The first day of the march, was late in the evening when we came to a farmhouse. And the farmhouse owner, with the farmer, he gave us potatoes, potatoes what he feeds the pigs. So we were happy to have that. And then he opened up the farm, a barn. He opened up a barn and we went in the barn. The barn was with straw. And while we were laying there to have rest, we noticed that there is lots of grain still on the, on the-

speaker: interviewer
text: On the wheat.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the wheat. And so we were all eating this grain, taking out grain, and eating the grain and was not too bad. And so the next day, we were feeling not too bad. And everybody had enough to eat, I guess. And we started to march again. The next night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS. The next evening we came to a farm house. And again, the same thing, but he gave us a different kind of vegetables and potatoes. And he had some carrots what he had there. And we had the carrots. And we had some potatoes. We had some other stuff too. So we ate that. But the barn was empty. There was no straw in it. So we laid out our blanket and we laid on the blanket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a very nice boy what was, I was always with him. We worked together. He was also electrician. And, and he didn't take off his shoes overnight. And when we woke up in the next morning, he said, I can't feel my feet. So I looked and I said oh, what are we gonna do? You froze your feet. And so a matter of fact, he had. And he couldn't almost couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to help him so on this-- we stayed further back and further back, till finally, the SS man says, rouse, go quicker, you know. And so I never answered, you know. So I went and went a little quicker and he fell. And he fell. He shot him. And he shot him, and he wanted to shoot me too. But I went zig zag. And I run forward and I run back into the rows. And I put on the, the blanket over my head, you know. And I went over on the other side. And he came and he looked, you see, he didn't see me. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were marching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, we were 2,300.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how many days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first night, we lost about 50. The next night, we lost about 100. The third night, in the morning, we lost more than that. And finally, by the time we came to Gross-Rosen, we were only approximately 900 people left of all the 2,300. And at Gross-Rosen, when we got there and it was February. And it was just before my birthday about a couple of weeks before. And it was terrible. It was raining, and snowing, and such a weather. And there was lots of mud all over the camp in Gross-Rosen. And when we marched in into the camp, and they showed us in which barrack we go, when I was walking, I felt something underneath my feet. Something is not right. And when I looked and I put the mud away, there were dead people. And we lost, over there, we must have lost about half of our 900 in the mud, and they never woke up in the next morning. And it was horrible. So we were in Gross-Rosen about three days. And I didn't mention, in Blechhammer, I got my number. In Blechhammer, they gave me that number-- 177867. This is my number from in Blechhammer. And from then on, we were only numbers. OK. And so in Gross-Rosen, we were about three days. And then from Gross-Rosen, they loaded us up in cattle cars, in open cattle cars, not closed, open cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the, with the, a soldier on, standing in that little platform to watch us. And they drove us from there all the way to Buchenwald. And by the time we got to Buchenwald, I don't think we were left with 400 people. 50 died on the road there. So from 2,500 people from Blechhammer, we came to, to Buchenwald maybe 300 people, less than that. And in Buchenwald, I was approximately let's say two weeks, and one day or two days after my birthday. And that was in 1945. I was 15 years old. No, I was 20. 1945, I was 20. Yeah. Yeah, I was 20 years old. Again, they came in and they called out my number that I go working again. OK. So assembled on the big place in the middle of Buchenwald. And by the time they called out the names and so on, we were five left. And so I said to the SS man, I said, we weren't called. So he looked at his, oh, ja, he said. Ja, I have five people what don't go. You go tomorrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by the way, he says, I know you are business people, huh? Jews are always business people. You, you gonna take all your clothes off. And you, because you gonna, when you go to the barrack to sleep overnight, you gonna sell it for you're make business with your clothes. So you get nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: You'll take all your clothes off. So and it was February, just before my birthday. It was a terrible wind. And it was a terrible rain, and with snow mixed. And there we were standing, five of us, nude on the middle of that place. And he laughing. And then he said, OK, you can go now. So the next day, we went again. And then we were the first five that were called. And they sent us to Bremen. We came to Bremen. Bremen, it was different. Brest is on the other side and Bremen is on this side. And they were making in the mountains, they had-- hills, big hills, wasn't too big of a mountain, but big hills-- they were building tunnels so they can repair airplanes and building V-1s and V-2s. And we were there about a week, and I got sick. I got sick with high fever. And the doctors, they gave me some medicine, antibiotics, what was possible to give, plus aspirins, and so. And I didn't go to work any more. And I was laying there in the sick barracks for about a week. And that was already beginning of March. They loaded us up into train, into cattle cars again-- this time, closed ones. And we went to, back to Buchenwald because the Americans, the English, they were already too close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were getting close.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they had to leave that what they builded there. And we went on the road. And while we were on the road, there was an alarm of flyers. Airplanes came and they shot all along the train, and the engine was completely demolished. So we were all sitting outside because they told us to jump out from the train into the ditch.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went back into the wagons, and we had to wait till they bring a new engine. So while we were there, and it was raining and the water was coming through the holes. And I was just laying there on the floor. And I could catch some water, so I drank from that rainwater. And well, so it took about a day, a night I guess. And they brought another engine and we went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by the time I came to Buchenwald, I not only had high fever and I had pneumonia, but I also had typhoid. And so in over there, they brought me to a barrack, what a special barrack for people who gonna be dying. I don't remember if that was the 13, 13, barrack 13, or barrack 17, something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in that barrack, and it got worse and worse with me. And that was already end of March. And April came along, and the 11th of April, I was liberated. And before I was liberated, I heard some noises again outside. But I was so weak and I was so terrible, that I said, I'm not gonna go no where. And I was, it was layers of platforms. And I was laying on the lowest level.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the night came, that was on the 10th of April, and everybody, they were yelling rouse, rouse, rouse, rouse. And I went out from this level and I crawled into the room where they threw the dead people every morning. And I went and I laid on top of the dead people. And on the 11th of April, when the Americans came in with the tanks, and they came in into these barracks, and one of the other were saying, holy mackerel, holy, look at, look at that, look. And then they came to the door, and they said, holy, holy, man, look, ah. And then one of them said, look at him. He's moving with his eyes. I knew English, because I learned English in school in Germany, so I understood what they were saying. And he said, look at he. And all I could do is move my eyes. And they had to take off about two or three people on top of me, and they hauled me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came right away with the, with the stretcher. They put me on a stretcher. And they put me in ambulance, in an American ambulance with [? straw ?] on it and everything. And they took me right away to the SS . hospital And they made it into an American hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at that hospital till July the first.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A day, they didn't give me nothing to eat. They gave me intravenous first, for the first week. And they gave me a lot of penicillin. Every two hours I got a needle of penicillin-- one here, one there, one here, and one there, one here, one there. And that went on for about two weeks, nothing but intravenous and penicillin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And oh, and you know, by the first when they brought me there, on the road in the ambulance, one of these [? sanitators, ?] the nurses, he said, is there something-- and he spoke German-- is there something what we can do for you, what you want? I said a cigarette.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you came out of the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came out from that hospital, they, the American officer, he gave us a bus, a German bus and a German driver. And we had the authority to tell him where to go to drop off the people. Some went to Belgium, some to Holland, and some went to France. And I wanted to go back to France. I wanted to go to Paris. I wanted to go and see if I can find my parents. And well, when we came to France, they took us away the, the bus. And so they put us on a train, and we went with the train to Paris. And in Paris, I found the OSE. That was a organization just for children. And I found my friends from Buchenwald all in the OSE. And I found out that we were about 50 people left over from the 2,500. Yeah, 50, 50 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, I found out right away in Paris that my father was deported in August 1943, and to Auschwitz. Well, and I know, he never came back. My mother, I couldn't find. I didn't know where she was. So a boy in the OSE, in the home, the youth home, he had an uncle in New York. And he remembered the address-- not exactly the address, but he remembered that he is from the larger group in Paris-- in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did I say Paris? Not Paris, in New York, In New York, they have groups from society, from every city in Poland. So he wrote to his uncle, he was from Lodz. And he asked if they have an organization from [? Gradna, ?] and my name, and my uncle's name, and everything. And so a months passed by, and I get a phone call from a lady. He said in Russian to me, [RUSSIAN] is that you? I said, Mom? Yeah. How did you found me? Oh, she said, somebody told your uncle in New York that you are in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he wrote, telegraphed, to his brother in Leningrad. And his brother in Leningrad had the address of grandma, of my mother. And my mother phoned me right away. And I phoned you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How wonderful. Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in Nancy, and she was married.
